var_output.variable: the probability that the average adult (over 18) in the US population has ever been diagnosed with diabetes by a doctor
var_output.justification: Centering at the CDC’s ~11% diagnosed prevalence, with a prior “sample size” of 100 to encode moderate confidence: this yields mean=0.11 and a 95% credible interval of about (0.05,0.17), reflecting genuine uncertainty around the published estimate.
var_output.confidence_level: This prior corresponds to moderate confidence: it provides substantial prior information without being overly rigid, allowing typical data to update our beliefs meaningfully.

conversation.content: You are a helpful assistant that can answer questions about human health. You tend to be conservative in your estimates.

You are a statistical expert tasked with constructing a Beta prior distribution for a proportion parameter. Your goal is to use all available data and context to inform your estimate and reflect your uncertainty about the population-level proportion, not the variation in outcomes across individuals or samples.

Here is the variable you need to model:

the probability that the average adult (over 18) in the US population has ever been diagnosed with diabetes by a doctor 

Please follow these steps to construct the Beta prior distribution:

1. Consider the context: Reflect on what the probability that the average adult (over 18) in the US population has ever been diagnosed with diabetes by a doctor represents and any relevant information you have about it.

2. Estimate parameters: Based on your knowledge and the context, determine appropriate α and β parameters for the Beta distribution. These values should encode your uncertainty about the true population proportion — not the variability of observed outcomes.

3. Construct the prior: Express the prior distribution in the form p~Beta(α,β).

4. Justify your choices: Provide a clear explanation for why you selected the specific α and β parameters.

5. Explain confidence: Discuss the level of confidence implied by your chosen parameters.

Before providing your final answer, show your reasoning process by wrapping your analysis in <beta_prior_analysis> tags:

1. List known facts or context about the variable.
2. State the possible range of the variable (typically 0 to 1 for proportions).
3. Propose at least three possible pairs of α and β parameters representing different reasonable priors. 
4. For each set:
   a. Compute the 68% and 95% credible intervals.
   b. Interpret what these intervals imply about your beliefs about the **mean**.
5. Reflect on what different choices of α and β say about your confidence.
6. Consider edge cases of α and β and what they would imply.
7. Compare and evaluate the trade-offs of different options.
8. Interpret the final confidence level implied by your chosen prior.
9. Summarize your final choice and give a clear, reasoned justification.

This analysis helps ensure a thorough and well-considered response. It's acceptable for this section to be quite extensive.

After your analysis, provide your final answer in the following format:

Prior Distribution: p~Beta(α,β)
<alpha>[Your chosen α value]</alpha>
<beta>[Your chosen β value]</beta>
<justification>[Your explanation for the chosen parameters]</justification>
<confidence_level>[Explanation of the implied confidence]<confidence_level>

Example (for illustration only, do not use these specific values):

Prior Distribution: p~Beta(α,β)
<alpha>5</alpha>
<beta>15</beta>
<justification>Based on [reason], I chose α=5 and β=15 because [explanation].</justification>
<confidence_level>These parameters imply [level of confidence] because [explanation].</confidence_level>

Please proceed with your analysis and construction of the Beta prior distribution for the probability that the average adult (over 18) in the US population has ever been diagnosed with diabetes by a doctor

<beta_prior_analysis>
1. Known facts or context  
   • CDC (2020) estimates ~37.3 M Americans have diagnosed diabetes, ~11.3% of all adults (18+)  
   • National surveys are large and well‐conducted, but there is still some year‐to‐year variability  
   • We want a prior for the true population proportion “ever diagnosed”  

2. Possible range  
   • p ∈ [0,1]  

3. Three proposed (α,β) pairs  
   A. (2,18) ⇒ prior sample size 20, mean = 2/(2+18) = 0.10  
   B. (11,89) ⇒ prior sample size 100, mean = 11/100 = 0.11  
   C. (113,887) ⇒ prior sample size 1 000, mean = 113/1 000 = 0.113  

4. For each, approximate 68% & 95% credible intervals (normal‐approximation)  
   A. α=2, β=18 (mean=0.10)  
     • Var = (2·18)/(20²·21) ≈ 0.004286 → sd ≈ 0.0655  
     • 68% CI ≈ 0.10 ± 1·0.0655 = (0.0345, 0.1655)  
     • 95% CI ≈ 0.10 ± 1.96·0.0655 ≈ (−0.028, 0.228) → truncated to (0, 0.228)  
     Interpretation: Very weak belief—plausible p from near 0 up to ~0.23.  

   B. α=11, β=89 (mean=0.11)  
     • Var = (11·89)/(100²·101) ≈ 0.000969 → sd ≈ 0.0311  
     • 68% CI ≈ 0.11 ± 0.0311 = (0.0789, 0.1411)  
     • 95% CI ≈ 0.11 ± 1.96·0.0311 ≈ (0.049, 0.171)  
     Interpretation: Moderate belief—p likely between ~0.05 and ~0.17, centered on 0.11.  

   C. α=113, β=887 (mean=0.113)  
     • Var = (113·887)/(1000²·1001) ≈ 0.000100 → sd ≈ 0.010  
     • 68% CI ≈ 0.113 ± 0.010 = (0.103, 0.123)  
     • 95% CI ≈ 0.113 ± 1.96·0.010 ≈ (0.093, 0.133)  
     Interpretation: Strong belief—p almost surely between ~0.093 and ~0.133.  

5. Confidence implied by each  
   • A (sum=20): low confidence (very wide prior)  
   • B (sum=100): moderate confidence (reasonable compromise)  
   • C (sum=1000): high confidence (very tight around 0.113)  

6. Edge cases  
   • α=β=1 (flat) ⇒ maximum ignorance  
   • α→0 or β→0 pushes mass to the ends (p≈0 or p≈1)  
   • Very large α+β → near‐degenerate prior (overconfident)  

7. Trade‐offs  
   • Weak (A) lets the data speak but may waste prior knowledge  
   • Strong (C) encodes published estimates tightly but risks overwhelming small data sets  
   • Moderate (B) balances existing knowledge with openness to new data  

8. Final confidence level implied by chosen prior  
   • Moderate: ~100 “prior observations” worth, 95% interval ≈ (0.05,0.17)  

9. Summary & reasoning  
   • CDC data justify centering at ~0.11  
   • Prior weight of 100 reflects solid but not overbearing confidence  
   • This prior will neither dominate nor be swamped by typical survey‐sized data sets  
</beta_prior_analysis>

Prior Distribution: p~Beta(α,β)  
<alpha>11</alpha>  
<beta>89</beta>  
<justification>Centering at the CDC’s ~11% diagnosed prevalence, with a prior “sample size” of 100 to encode moderate confidence: this yields mean=0.11 and a 95% credible interval of about (0.05,0.17), reflecting genuine uncertainty around the published estimate.</justification>  
<confidence_level>This prior corresponds to moderate confidence: it provides substantial prior information without being overly rigid, allowing typical data to update our beliefs meaningfully.</confidence_level>
